A.name: Aho, Lam, Sethi & Ullman
A.title: Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools
A.desc: Known as "the Dragon Book," this is the standard reference on how compilers are built, covering everything from lexical analysis through code generation and optimization.

D.name: Dirac
D.title: The Principles of Quantum Mechanics
D.desc: A foundational, axiomatic treatment of quantum theory built around bra-ket notation, written by Nobel laureate Paul Dirac; long considered one of the classic texts in physics.

K.name: Knuth
K.title: The Art of Computer Programming
K.desc: Donald Knuth’s monumental, still-unfinished series analyzing algorithms and data structures with mathematical rigor; a cornerstone reference in computer science.

B.name: Majidy, Wilson & Laflamme
B.title: Building Quantum Computers: A Practical Introduction
B.desc: A hardware-focused textbook comparing the leading physical platforms for quantum computing: nuclear magnetic resonance, optics, trapped ions, and superconducting circuits.

M.name: Manenti & Motta
M.title: Quantum Information Science
M.desc: An introduction bridging the math, physics, and engineering of quantum computing, written by an industry quantum engineer and a research scientist for students starting graduate study.

N.name: Nielsen & Chuang
N.title: Quantum Computation and Quantum Information
N.desc: One of the most cited books in physics and the standard reference for the field, covering quantum algorithms, error-correction, and quantum information theory.

S.name: Sakurai & Napolitano
S.title: Modern Quantum Mechanics
S.desc: A widely used graduate quantum mechanics text originally by J. J. Sakurai and completed after his death, later revised and extended by Jim Napolitano with modern applications.
